content:
# Final Review Proposal
**Producer:** agent:claude/claude-opus-4-6

## Summary

The test infrastructure implementation is solid and well-structured. Mock br/bv adapters correctly implement their respective ProcessRunner/BvProcessRunner traits with full call tracking, response queueing, and latency simulation. Fixture builders produce realistic milestone and bead state through production service functions (not raw file writes), which is the right approach. The log capture helper correctly uses OnceLock for process-wide singleton initialization, and the assert_event_has_fields diagnostic output is excellent. The refactoring of agent_execution_test.rs to use shared log_capture() and run_attach_tmux.rs to use TempWorkspaceBuilder successfully eliminates duplication. All 753 tests pass. Two issues worth addressing: (1) the test_support module ships in production builds because it lacks a feature gate, and (2) the env-mutation helpers will break on Rust edition 2024 due to #![forbid(unsafe_code)].

## Amendments

1. The `test_support` module is compiled unconditionally into the production library (`pub mod test_support` in `src/lib.rs` without any `#[cfg(test)]` or feature gate). This is necessary because integration tests in `tests/` reference it via `ralph_burning::test_support`, and `#[cfg(test)]` items are invisible to integration test crates. However, this means the mock adapters, fixture builders, `tempfile` usage, and env-mutation helpers are included in release builds. Consider gating the module behind a cargo feature (e.g., `#[cfg(feature = "test-support")]`) that `[dev-dependencies]` activates, or behind `#[cfg(any(test, feature = "test-stub"))]` since a `test-stub` feature already exists in Cargo.toml. This prevents shipping test infrastructure in the production binary.
   Rationale: Shipping mock adapters and fixture builders in production increases binary size and attack surface. The existing `test-stub` feature in Cargo.toml suggests the project already has a pattern for test-only compilation gates. The `tempfile` crate is currently a regular dependency (not dev-dependency), which compounds this — fixture code that creates temp dirs is always compiled in.
2. In `src/test_support/env.rs`, the `OpenRouterKeyGuard` and `PathGuard` types use `std::env::set_var` and `std::env::remove_var`, which are safe in Rust edition 2021 but will become `unsafe fn` in edition 2024 (stabilized in Rust 1.83+). Since these functions mutate process-wide state and are unsound when called concurrently with other env-reading code, and the crate declares `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` in `lib.rs`, upgrading to edition 2024 will cause compilation failures in this module. Add a comment documenting this known limitation, or consider wrapping these calls in `unsafe {}` blocks with safety justifications preemptively, so the migration path is clear.
   Rationale: The `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` at the crate root will conflict with `unsafe` env mutations when the crate eventually upgrades to edition 2024. The mutex serialization guards mitigate the data race in practice, but the compiler will still require `unsafe` blocks. Flagging this now prevents a surprise blocker during edition migration.
